summary: This advice column covers relationship dynamics, career positioning in data science, graduate school anxiety, economic inequality discourse, and health stereotypes. Aunt Pythia advises a woman whose partner has gained weight to pursue an open relationship rather than expect weight loss. She suggests data scientists belong in a third space outside IT or business, dismisses an economist's inequality arguments as smug rationalization, and challenges stereotypes about fat women and health.
key_points: Weight loss through dieting has such poor long-term success rates that expecting a partner to lose weight is 'useless' as a relationship strategy
Data scientists should occupy a third space that translates between IT and business rather than belonging to either
Graduate school in math involves constantly feeling like an idiot - that discomfort is what learning feels like
Some economists use 'fancy language to avoid the very simple concept of fairness' when discussing inequality
Physical health and weight are not as correlated as stereotypes suggest - exercise levels and health metrics can be excellent regardless of body size
money_quotes: What you don't say is that if she loses 100 pounds you will be faithful. That would be hurtful and, if you've ever examined dieting data, useless.
In some sense the modern data scientist's job is to translate between those two worlds without being in either of them.
My general feeling is that this man has spent a lifetime figuring out how to use fancy language to avoid the very simple concept of fairness.
Sounds pretty smug to me, almost like an invitation.
Keep feeling like an idiot, because that's what learning feels like.
I am, happily, a counter example to the tired stereotype of the lazy fat woman. I have never been lazy, and my weight has basically nothing to do with my exercise levels.
How do you recognize a good, honest hedge fund? Hahahahahaha! Good one.
key_insight: Seemingly personal problems often have structural dimensions - whether it's the futility of dieting, the organizational misplacement of data scientists, or economists using sophisticated language to rationalize inequality.
